content.problemStatement:
Consider an array AAA with NNN positive integers.

Let BBB be defined as an array of NNN integers such that for each 1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N:


Bi=Ai−1B_i = A_i - 1Bi​=Ai​−1, if AiA_iAi​ is even.
Bi=Ai+1B_i = A_i + 1Bi​=Ai​+1, if AiA_iAi​ is odd.
You are given an array CCC of size 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N which is formed by taking all the elements of the arrays AAA and BBB and rearranging them.
You need to find the number of distinct arrays AAA whose sum of elements is KKK, from which CCC can be obtained.

**Note: Two arrays XXX and YYY are considered to be distinct if there exists no rearrangement of XXX which is the same as YYY.**

Since the final answer can be very large, print the answer modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK - the parameters mentioned in the statement.
The second line of each test case contains 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N space-separated integers C1,C2,…,C2NC_1, C_2, \dots, C_{2N}C1​,C2​,…,C2N​ denoting the array CCC.


Output Format
For each test case, output the number of distinct arrays AAA such that the sum of its elements is KKK. Print the answer modulo (109+710^9 + 7109+7).
constraints: Sum of NNN over all the test cases does not exceed 10310^3103.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: If A=[1,3]A = [1, 3]A=[1,3], then B=[2,4]B = [2, 4]B=[2,4]. C=[1,2,3,4]C = [1, 2, 3, 4]C=[1,2,3,4] can be obtained by rearranging the elements of AAA and BBB. It can be proven that A=[1,3]A = [1, 3]A=[1,3] is the only such distinct array whose elements sum to 444. Thus the answer is 111.
Note that arrays [1,3][1, 3][1,3] and [3,1][3, 1][3,1] are not distinct.
Test case 2:

If A=[2,3]A = [2, 3]A=[2,3], then B=[1,4]B = [1, 4]B=[1,4]. C=[1,2,3,4]C = [1, 2, 3, 4]C=[1,2,3,4] can be obtained by rearranging the elements of AAA and BBB.
If A=[1,4]A = [1, 4]A=[1,4], then B=[2,3]B = [2, 3]B=[2,3]. C=[1,2,3,4]C = [1, 2, 3, 4]C=[1,2,3,4] can be obtained by rearranging the elements of AAA and BBB.

It can be proven that [2,3][2, 3][2,3] and [1,4][1, 4][1,4] are the only such distinct arrays whose elements sum to 555. Thus the answer is 222.
